findings.summary: preflight._check_llm_reachable() calls openai.models.list() on the module-level openai default client instead of the explicitly-configured client held in common.llm._openai_holder. This bypasses the trust_env=False httpx.Client configured in library_setup and creates an implicit dependency on openai module-level state rather than the shared singleton boundary.
evidence: preflight.py line 73: openai.models.list() uses the default openai module client
library_setup.py lines 35-40: A custom OpenAI client is created with trust_env=False and stored in _openai_holder
llm.py lines 34-38: _openai_holder.get() is the sanctioned way to access the configured client
findings.suggestion: Have _check_llm_reachable() use the shared client from _openai_holder.get() (or accept it as a parameter) so the preflight check uses the same HTTP configuration as production calls.

findings.summary: OCR refusal/error phrases are defined in two independent locations: Settings.OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS (configurable via env var, used by OcrProvider) and classifier.constants.ERROR_PHRASES (hardcoded frozen tuple, used by quality_gates.needs_error_tag). These overlap substantially but drift independently, creating a risk that the classifier and OCR daemon disagree on what constitutes an error.
evidence: config.py lines 93-105: OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS includes 'i can't assist', 'i cannot assist', REFUSAL_MARK, etc.
constants.py lines 47-53: ERROR_PHRASES includes 'i'm sorry, i can't assist with that.', 'chatgpt refused to transcribe', etc.
The two lists have overlapping but not identical entries and are maintained independently
findings.suggestion: Consolidate error phrases into a single source of truth in common (e.g., in config or a shared constants module) so both daemons use the same definition. The classifier can still add domain-specific phrases if needed, but the base set should be shared.

findings.summary: The classifier daemon's _requeue_for_ocr method directly references settings.PRE_TAG_ID (the OCR queue tag), creating a cross-domain dependency where the classifier module must know about the OCR pipeline's tag semantics to re-queue documents.
evidence: classifier/worker.py line 195: updated.add(self.settings.PRE_TAG_ID) -- this is the OCR pre-processing tag
The classifier module should ideally not need to know about OCR-specific tag IDs
findings.suggestion: This is a minor coupling since Settings is already the shared configuration boundary. If the two daemons are always co-deployed, this is acceptable. For stronger separation, a dedicated requeue helper in common could encapsulate this knowledge.

findings.summary: The OCR daemon defines _process_document as a module-level function in daemon.py that creates per-document PaperlessClient and OcrProvider instances. The classifier daemon defines an equivalent process_document as a closure inside main(). These serve identical roles but use different structural patterns.
evidence: ocr/daemon.py lines 18-26: _process_document is a module-level function taking (doc, settings)
classifier/daemon.py lines 66-75: process_document is a nested closure inside main() capturing settings, taxonomy_cache from enclosing scope
findings.suggestion: Align both daemon modules to use the same pattern. The closure pattern in the classifier is justified because it captures the shared taxonomy_cache, but the inconsistency is worth noting. A factory function or a shared base pattern could unify them.

findings.summary: Both OcrProvider.transcribe_image() and ClassificationProvider.classify_text() call self._stats.reset() at the start of every invocation, meaning stats only reflect the most recent call rather than accumulating. In a concurrent thread-pool context, this means one thread's reset can zero out counters mid-flight for another thread processing a different page/document.
evidence: ocr/provider.py line 65: self._stats.reset(self._STAT_KEYS) at the start of transcribe_image
classifier/provider.py line 130: self._stats.reset(self._STAT_KEYS) at the start of classify_text

Each provider is instantiated per-document in the daemon, so inter-document races are avoided, but the OCR provider processes multiple pages concurrently via ThreadPoolExecutor -- stats reset before page 1 can wipe stats from a concurrent page
findings.suggestion: Move stats reset to the call site (worker) or create per-invocation stats objects rather than resetting shared mutable state that may be accessed concurrently.

findings.summary: common/utils.py contains only two functions (contains_redacted_marker and is_error_content) that are specifically about OCR error/refusal detection. The module name 'utils' is generic and does not convey its focused purpose.
evidence: utils.py contains only contains_redacted_marker() and is_error_content() -- both are about error/refusal detection
The module docstring says 'Redaction-marker and refusal-phrase detection for OCR output'
findings.suggestion: Rename to a more descriptive name like common/error_detection.py or common/refusal_detection.py to match the focused content. The current 'utils' name invites unrelated additions over time.

findings.summary: The src/paperless_ocr.egg-info/ directory is committed (or at least present) in the source tree alongside the actual packages. This build artifact should typically be excluded from the repository.
evidence: Glob results show src/paperless_ocr.egg-info/ with PKG-INFO, SOURCES.txt, etc. present in the working tree
findings.suggestion: Add paperless_ocr.egg-info/ to .gitignore and remove from version control if tracked.

findings.summary: conftest.py defines two Settings fixtures with different naming conventions: 'settings' (returns a real Settings instance) and 'settings_obj' (returns a MagicMock). The '_obj' suffix is non-standard and the distinction between real vs mock is not obvious from the names. Both are Settings-like objects.
evidence: conftest.py line 36: @pytest.fixture settings() calls make_settings() -- returns real Settings
conftest.py line 43: @pytest.fixture settings_obj() calls make_settings_obj() -- returns MagicMock
The naming does not clearly distinguish real vs mock behavior
findings.suggestion: Rename to 'real_settings' and 'mock_settings' (or 'settings' and 'mock_settings') to make the distinction explicit. The factory functions in factories.py should follow the same renaming.

findings.summary: The test helper _make_processor functions in test_worker.py (OCR) and test_worker.py (classifier) use different parameter conventions. The OCR version uses direct keyword arguments for settings overrides; the classifier version uses a settings_overrides dict parameter.
evidence: tests/unit/ocr/test_worker.py line 16-32: _make_processor(doc=None, paperless=None, ocr_provider=None, settings=None, **setting_overrides)
tests/unit/classifier/test_worker.py line 17-59: _make_processor(doc=None, settings_overrides=None, paperless_overrides=None, classifier_overrides=None, taxonomy_overrides=None)
findings.suggestion: Standardize both helpers to use the same convention. The classifier pattern (explicit *_overrides dict params) is more flexible when there are many dependency types; adopt it for both.

findings.summary: Both e2e test files (test_ocr_workflow.py and test_classifier_workflow.py) define their own _make_stateful_client helper with nearly identical implementation. This duplicated test infrastructure deviates from the pattern of centralizing test helpers in tests/helpers/.
evidence: test_ocr_workflow.py lines 42-73: _make_stateful_client with tag state tracking
test_classifier_workflow.py lines 58-114: _make_stateful_client with tag state tracking (same pattern, extended with taxonomy stubs)
findings.suggestion: Extract the shared stateful client builder into tests/helpers/mocks.py with optional taxonomy stubs, then import it in both e2e test files.

findings.summary: The _make_png_bytes helper function is duplicated across test_ocr_pipeline.py (integration) and test_ocr_workflow.py (e2e) with identical implementations.
evidence: test_ocr_pipeline.py lines 14-19: _make_png_bytes(width, height, color) -> bytes
test_ocr_workflow.py lines 14-19: identical _make_png_bytes(width, height, color) -> bytes
findings.suggestion: Move _make_png_bytes into tests/helpers/factories.py and import it in both test files.

findings.summary: The common/ package contains 13 modules spanning concerns from config and logging to tag lifecycle, claims, document iteration, and LLM wiring. While each module is individually focused, the package itself has no internal decomposition, making it a catch-all for anything shared. A new engineer cannot predict where a given concern lives without scanning all files.
evidence: 13 modules in src/common/ with no sub-packages
tags.py, claims.py, stale_lock.py, and document_iter.py all deal with the tag-based processing lifecycle but sit alongside unrelated modules like llm.py and logging_config.py
bootstrap.py imports 7 sibling modules from common/ — a sign the package has become a hub
findings.suggestion: Consider grouping tag-lifecycle modules (tags.py, claims.py, stale_lock.py, document_iter.py) into a common/pipeline sub-package to give navigational structure without changing APIs.

findings.summary: ocr/daemon.py and classifier/daemon.py follow identical structural patterns (bootstrap, log startup params, define process_document closure, call run_polling_threadpool, close clients in finally) but with no shared abstraction. The two daemons differ only in which provider/processor they instantiate and which tag IDs they use.
evidence: Both daemon.py files: bootstrap_daemon() -> log.info('Starting...') -> try: run_polling_threadpool(...) finally: client.close()
Both define a process_document function that creates a PaperlessClient, a provider, a processor, calls processor.process(), and closes the client in finally
classifier/daemon.py adds only taxonomy_cache and before_each_batch as differences
findings.suggestion: A small shared daemon runner function or base class that accepts a factory for the processor/provider could eliminate the structural duplication while keeping domain-specific wiring in each package.

findings.summary: OcrProvider.transcribe_image resets ThreadSafeStats at the start of every call (self._stats.reset(self._STAT_KEYS)). Since the provider is instantiated per-document and transcribe_image is called per-page, this means stats from page 1 are wiped before page 2 runs. The final stats snapshot returned by get_stats() only reflects the last page processed, not the full document.
evidence: provider.py line 65: self._stats.reset(self._STAT_KEYS) — called at the start of transcribe_image
worker.py calls transcribe_image once per page in _ocr_pages_in_parallel, then calls get_stats() once at the end
Only the last page's stats survive, so aggregate doc-level stats (total attempts, total refusals) are lost
findings.suggestion: Move the stats reset to the document level (e.g. in worker.py before the page loop starts) or remove the per-call reset entirely since the provider is already per-document.

findings.summary: PaperlessClient._create_named_item raises AssertionError (typo for AssertionError) on an unreachable path. While it is marked pragma: no cover, the typo suggests the error was never exercised even manually.
evidence: paperless.py line 117: raise AssertionError(... — should be AssertionError; Python will raise NameError at runtime if this path is ever reached
findings.suggestion: Fix the typo to AssertionError or replace with a plain RuntimeError/AssertionError.

findings.summary: _check_llm_reachable() in preflight.py uses the module-level openai.models.list() which relies on the module-level default openai client, not the explicitly configured client stored in _OpenAIClientHolder. This means the preflight check may succeed/fail based on environment variables rather than the actual configured client the daemons will use.
evidence: preflight.py line 73: openai.models.list() — uses the default module-level openai client
library_setup.py creates a custom OpenAI client with explicit http_client and base_url, stored via init_openai_client()
The preflight check bypasses this configured client entirely
findings.suggestion: Use the holder's client (_openai_holder.get().models.list()) so the preflight check exercises the same client the daemons will use for real work.

findings.summary: Tags are repeatedly converted between set[int] and list[int] at module boundaries. Functions like extract_tags return set[int], but the Paperless API requires list[int], so callers scatter list(...) conversions. This creates visual noise and subtle ordering concerns (sets are unordered).
evidence: tags.py: extract_tags returns set[int]; remove_stale_queue_tag calls client.update_document_metadata(tags=list(updated))
claims.py line 53: client.update_document_metadata(doc_id, tags=list(updated_tags))
worker.py (ocr) line 202: client.update_document(self.doc_id, full_text, list(current_tags))
worker.py (classifier) line 299: tags=list(current_tags)
At least 8 occurrences of list(...) wrapping a set of tags across the codebase
findings.suggestion: Consider having the update methods accept sets directly and do the conversion once internally, or use sorted(tags) for deterministic ordering in tests and logs.

findings.summary: The retry decorator's wrapper function has an implicit None return after the for loop exhausts without raising. If all retries are consumed and the exception is raised on the last attempt (line 46), this is correct. But if the retryable exception check somehow doesn't match on the last iteration, the function falls through and returns None with no type error.
evidence: retry.py lines 32-56: wrapper function has no explicit return after the for loop. If the loop completes without returning (e.g., MAX_RETRIES somehow yields 0 iterations despite the >= 1 check), the return type annotation Callable[..., T] is violated.
findings.suggestion: Add a defensive raise or return after the for loop to make the control flow exhaustive and satisfy type checkers.

findings.summary: ClassificationProvider._try_strip_compat_param dispatches error detection via getattr(self, detector_name)(error), using string-based method names stored in the _COMPAT_PARAMS tuple. This indirection makes it harder to trace which detector runs for which parameter and defeats IDE navigation.
evidence: provider.py line 62-66: _COMPAT_PARAMS stores method names as strings: ('temperature', '_is_temperature_error', 'temperature_retries')
provider.py line 101: getattr(self, detector_name)(error) — dynamic dispatch by string name
findings.suggestion: Store direct callable references (e.g., (self._is_temperature_error,)) in _COMPAT_PARAMS or use a list of dataclass/NamedTuple entries with callable fields to preserve traceability.

findings.summary: OCR refusal detection phrases are defined in two separate places with overlapping but not identical content: Settings._load_llm_settings() (OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS default list) and classifier/constants.py (ERROR_PHRASES tuple). Both are used for the same purpose — detecting model refusals — but neither references the other.
evidence: config.py lines 93-99: default_ocr_refusal_markers includes 'i can't assist', 'i cannot assist', 'chatgpt refused to transcribe', etc.
constants.py lines 47-53: ERROR_PHRASES includes 'i'm sorry, i can't assist with that', 'chatgpt refused to transcribe', 'i can't help with transcrib', etc.
Both are passed to the same is_error_content() function in common/utils.py
findings.suggestion: Define refusal phrases once in a shared location (e.g., common/constants.py) and import from both the config defaults and classifier quality gates.

findings.summary: Both DocumentProcessor.process() (OCR) and ClassificationProcessor.process() have deep nesting (try/finally wrapping try/except wrapping if/return chains) with multiple early-return paths inside a single method. The OCR worker's process() is 75 lines with 4 levels of nesting at the deepest point.
evidence: ocr/worker.py process(): try -> if error_tag -> return, claim -> if not claimed -> return, download -> try convert -> except -> return, if not images -> return, try ocr -> finally close images, assemble, update
classifier/worker.py process(): try -> if error_tag -> return, claim -> if not claimed -> return, if not content -> return, truncate, classify, if empty -> return, if generic -> return, apply
findings.suggestion: Extract the inner phases (download-convert, ocr-pages, assemble-update) into separate methods that return early results, reducing the try/finally nesting depth in the main process() orchestrator.

findings.summary: Exception catch tuples vary across modules without a shared constant. Some catch (OSError, httpx.HTTPError), others catch (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, ValueError), and daemon_loop catches a broad tuple of five types. This makes it easy to miss a case or catch too broadly.
evidence: tags.py get_latest_tags catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, ValueError) at line 41
claims.py catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError) at lines 31, 54, 65
stale_lock.py catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError) at lines 27, 54
daemon_loop.py catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, RuntimeError, ValueError, TypeError) at line 106
findings.suggestion: Define a shared PAPERLESS_RETRYABLE_EXCEPTIONS tuple in common/ and reference it everywhere. The daemon_loop catch is especially broad and should be reviewed to determine if RuntimeError/TypeError are truly expected there.

findings.summary: paperless.py line 117 raises 'AssertionError' (misspelled) instead of 'AssertionError'. This is actually 'AssertionError' which is not a built-in; it would raise NameError at runtime. However since it is behind pragma: no cover and supposedly unreachable, it has not been caught.
evidence: Line 117: raise AssertionError(  # pragma: no cover
findings.suggestion: Fix the typo: change 'AssertionError' to 'AssertionError'. Actually review the code - if the intent is AssertionError that is correct Python. Let me re-examine: the code reads 'AssertionError' which IS a typo for 'AssertionError'. Wait - looking more carefully at the exact text: 'raise AssertionError(' - this IS a typo. It should be 'AssertionError'. Replace with a standard RuntimeError or fix the spelling.

findings.summary: preflight.py _check_llm_reachable() calls openai.models.list() using the module-level openai client, not the initialized singleton from _openai_holder. This bypasses the configured client (including base_url for Ollama) and could fail or hit the wrong endpoint.
evidence: preflight.py line 73: openai.models.list() - uses module-level default client
library_setup.py creates a configured OpenAI client and stores it in _openai_holder, but preflight doesn't use it
findings.suggestion: Use the initialized client from _openai_holder.get().models.list() instead of the module-level openai.models.list(). This ensures the preflight check hits the correct endpoint (especially important for Ollama users).

findings.summary: OcrProvider.transcribe_image() resets stats at the start of every call (line 65), which means stats only reflect the last page transcribed. For multi-page documents, accumulated stats across all pages are lost.
evidence: provider.py line 65: self._stats.reset(self._STAT_KEYS) inside transcribe_image()
worker.py line 226: _log_ocr_stats() calls self.ocr_provider.get_stats() after all pages are done, but stats only reflect the last page
findings.suggestion: Move the stats reset to the start of the document processing (e.g., in DocumentProcessor.process()) or remove the per-call reset so stats accumulate across all pages of a document.

findings.summary: The _make_stateful_client() helper is duplicated nearly identically in both e2e test files (test_ocr_workflow.py and test_classifier_workflow.py). This is test code but represents a maintenance burden.
evidence: test_ocr_workflow.py lines 42-73: _make_stateful_client()
test_classifier_workflow.py lines 58-113: _make_stateful_client() with same core logic plus taxonomy stubs
findings.suggestion: Extract the shared stateful client logic into tests/helpers/mocks.py alongside the existing mock builders.

findings.summary: _OpenAIClientHolder.init() and .get() have no synchronization. In theory, if init() is called while another thread calls get(), the state could be inconsistent. In practice bootstrap runs single-threaded before workers start, but the class name 'Thread-safe holder' in the docstring is misleading.
evidence: llm.py lines 21-38: _OpenAIClientHolder with no lock
llm.py line 22: docstring says 'Thread-safe holder'
findings.suggestion: Either add a lock to match the docstring claim, or correct the docstring to note that init must be called before any concurrent get() calls.

findings.summary: preflight.py imports and calls openai.models.list() at module level scope, creating a hard dependency on the openai module even for the preflight check. This also means preflight uses the unconfigured default openai client rather than the one set up in library_setup.
evidence: preflight.py line 7: import openai
preflight.py line 73: openai.models.list() - calls the default module-level client
findings.suggestion: Pass the configured OpenAI client (or a callable) into the preflight check rather than using the module-level default.

findings.summary: Some modules use TYPE_CHECKING guards for Settings imports (tags.py, library_setup.py) while others import it directly at runtime (config.py users everywhere). The TYPE_CHECKING usage in tags.py is needed because Settings is only used in type annotations, but library_setup.py guards a runtime-used import under TYPE_CHECKING with path 'common.config' while other modules use '.config'.
evidence: library_setup.py line 14-15: if TYPE_CHECKING: from common.config import Settings (absolute path)
tags.py line 5-6: if TYPE_CHECKING: from .config import Settings (relative path)
Other common/ modules use 'from .config import Settings' at runtime
findings.suggestion: Standardize import paths. Use relative imports consistently within the common package. The TYPE_CHECKING guard in library_setup.py uses an absolute path that may not resolve correctly depending on how the package is installed.

findings.summary: The OCR provider and classification provider have unit test files but they primarily test through mocks at the worker level. Direct unit tests for OcrProvider.transcribe_image() and ClassificationProvider.classify_text() with mocked OpenAI responses are thin compared to the complexity of these classes (model fallback chains, refusal detection, compat parameter stripping).
evidence: Provider classes have complex multi-model fallback logic, refusal detection, and parameter compatibility retry logic
Most provider behavior is tested indirectly through worker tests or not at all
findings.suggestion: Add direct unit tests for the provider classes with mocked _create_completion, covering: model fallback chains, refusal detection with various markers, the _create_with_compat parameter stripping loop, and edge cases like all models failing.

findings.summary: test_config.py has ~90 individual test methods, many following the exact same pattern of _build(mocker, env) followed by a single assertion. The TestDefaults class alone has 30 nearly identical tests that could be parametrized into a single test with a data table.
evidence: TestDefaults: 30 methods each calling _build(mocker, _MINIMAL_ENV) then asserting one attribute
TestCustomEnvVars: 28 methods each following the same pattern
All follow identical structure: build settings, assert one value
findings.suggestion: Use @pytest.mark.parametrize with a list of (env_overrides, attribute, expected_value) tuples to collapse repetitive test methods. This reduces boilerplate by ~60% while maintaining the same coverage.

findings.summary: The daemon processes documents concurrently via ThreadPoolExecutor, but there are no tests that exercise concurrent processing of multiple documents simultaneously. The one thread safety test in test_taxonomy.py only tests cache refresh, not the full concurrent processing path.
evidence: daemon_loop.py uses ThreadPoolExecutor with max_workers for concurrent doc processing
test_daemon_loop.py uses max_workers=1 in all tests, never testing actual concurrency
findings.suggestion: Add at least one test with max_workers>1 and multiple items to verify that concurrent processing does not corrupt shared state (e.g., ThreadSafeStats, LLMConcurrencyLimiter).

findings.summary: Several trivial one-liner or two-liner functions still carry multi-line docstrings that restate what the code already says clearly. While the cleanup removed some redundant docstrings, a number remain.
evidence: shutdown.py: register_signal_handlers docstring 'Install SIGTERM/SIGINT handlers. Must be called from the main thread.' - acceptable
concurrency.py line 29: acquire() has docstring 'Acquire a slot; no-op when unlimited.' for a 6-line context manager - borderline
quality_gates.py is_generic_document_type has a 4-line docstring for a 3-line function
findings.suggestion: This is minor after cleanup. The remaining docstrings are generally useful. No action needed unless further trimming is desired.

findings.summary: Test files consistently use '# Arrange', '# Act', '# Assert' section comments in nearly every test method. While this is a valid style, the mechanical consistency across 50+ test files suggests AI generation. Many tests are short enough that these comments add visual noise without clarity.
evidence: test_paperless.py: '# Arrange', '# Act', '# Assert' in every test method
test_tags.py: same pattern in every test
test_worker.py: same pattern throughout
findings.suggestion: Consider removing these section comments from tests shorter than ~10 lines where the arrange/act/assert structure is already obvious from the code itself.

findings.summary: Test files use many single-method or two-method test classes (e.g., TestInvalidLLMProvider with one test, TestInvalidLogFormat with one test, TestInvalidMaxRetries with two tests). This creates unnecessary class overhead. Grouping related single-method tests into broader classes or using plain functions would reduce scaffolding.
evidence: test_config.py has 17 test classes, 8 of which contain only 1-2 tests
TestInvalidLLMProvider: 1 method, TestInvalidLogFormat: 1 method, TestInvalidMaxRetries: 2 methods, TestInvalidMaxRetryBackoff: 2 methods
findings.suggestion: Consolidate single-method test classes into a broader 'TestConfigValidation' class, or use plain test functions. This reduces visual noise without losing test organization.
